skill.name: amazon-product-api-skill
skill.description: Extract structured Amazon search result data through the BrowserAct Amazon Product API workflow, including titles, ASINs, prices, ratings, and product attributes.
skill.summary: Extract Amazon product listings and details with BrowserAct API automation.
skill.author: browser-act

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified network access and environment variable access used to call the BrowserAct Amazon Product API. The external command and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from markdown examples and the term API Key, but the skill still sends user search data to a third-party service and requires a secret token.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party API Request Sends User Search Data
medium_findings.description: The script posts user-provided keywords, brand, page count, and language to the BrowserAct workflow API. This is expected behavior, but users should understand that search requests leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoint and payload construction are explicit, and the values come from command-line user inputs. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is disclosure and consent rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: API Key Read From Environment And Sent As Bearer Token
medium_findings.description: The script reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY from the environment and sends it in the Authorization header to BrowserAct. This is legitimate credential use, but it requires careful handling and should not be pasted into chat logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable and Authorization header are directly visible in the reviewed files. The target is the documented BrowserAct API, so the finding is high confidence but moderate severity.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Example Misclassified As Code Execution
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes a shell command showing how to run the local Python script. No dynamic command construction or shell execution is implemented in the Python code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are part of a fenced markdown usage example. The executable file uses requests and sys.argv, with no subprocess, eval, or shell invocation found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: Static alerts for weak cryptography appear to be triggered by product parameter names and API key wording. No hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic operation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe keywords, brand fields, page counts, language, and API keys. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or another weak cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Response Handling False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines inspect API responses and task status rather than collecting local system information. No evidence found of host, process, network interface, or filesystem reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script checks for Invalid authorization and task identifiers from BrowserAct responses. The lines do not access local machine metadata or enumerate the execution environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Plus Credential Access Requires User Consent
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines outbound network requests with a bearer token read from BROWSERACT_API_KEY. This is required for the advertised service but should be disclosed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combination is explicit in the Python script and semantically consistent with a third-party API client. No evidence found that credentials are sent anywhere other than the configured BrowserAct endpoint.

content.value_statement: Manual Amazon product research is slow and inconsistent across pages. This skill uses BrowserAct API automation to return structured product listing details for analysis.
actual_capabilities: Runs a local Python script that calls the BrowserAct workflow API.
Searches Amazon products by keyword and optional brand filter.
Requests pagination depth and interface language as input parameters.
Polls BrowserAct task status until completion or timeout.
Returns product listing fields such as title, ASIN, URL, price, rating, and attributes.
Detects missing or invalid BrowserAct API key errors and reports them.
limitations: Requires a valid BROWSERACT_API_KEY environment variable.
Sends search inputs and requests to the BrowserAct service.
Depends on BrowserAct workflow availability and Amazon result behavior.
The reviewed script has a completion-flow bug that can prevent result retrieval after a finished task.

use_cases.title: Compare competitor prices
use_cases.description: Collect Amazon listings for a brand or keyword to compare current prices, original prices, ratings, and featured badges.

use_cases.title: Build a product research dataset
use_cases.description: Extract ASINs, product URLs, titles, and attributes for a repeatable market research workflow.

use_cases.title: Review localized product results
use_cases.description: Run product searches with a selected language setting to inspect Amazon listings for international research.

prompt_templates.title: Search a product keyword
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Amazon product skill to search for wireless earbuds with the default brand and one page of results.

prompt_templates.title: Filter by brand
prompt_templates.scenario: Narrow a product search to a specific brand.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Amazon for laptops from Dell, scan one results page, and summarize price ranges and rating counts.

prompt_templates.title: Compare brands across pages
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Amazon for noise cancelling headphones for Sony across two pages and identify common price points and top-rated listings.

prompt_templates.title: Research localized listings
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced market research with language control.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Amazon for coffee makers from DeLonghi across two pages using German language settings, then summarize ASINs, prices, ratings, and notable attributes.

output_examples.input: Search for wireless earbuds from Samsung.
output_examples.output: A structured product summary with product titles, ASINs, Amazon URLs, current prices, ratings, rating counts, and any featured badges.

output_examples.input: Compare laptop listings for Dell across two pages.
output_examples.output: A comparison-ready result describing each listing, price ranges, rating strength, and visible product attributes such as style or material.

output_examples.input: Find high-rated coffee makers in German language results.
output_examples.output: A localized product list with titles, brand matches, ASINs, prices, ratings, and notes about available product specifications.

best_practices: Set BROWSERACT_API_KEY as an environment variable before running the script.
Start with one page of results, then increase pagination only when needed.
Review BrowserAct and Amazon terms before collecting or reusing product data.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys into shared prompts, logs, or issue reports.
Do not use broad searches when a brand or category filter is available.
Do not assume returned prices or ratings remain current after the request finishes.

faq.question: Does this skill need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires BROWSERACT_API_KEY to call the BrowserAct workflow API.

faq.question: Does it scrape Amazon directly from my machine?
faq.answer: No. The script sends workflow requests to BrowserAct, which performs the automated browsing task.

faq.question: What product fields can it return?
faq.answer: It can return titles, ASINs, product URLs, brands, prices, ratings, rating counts, badges, colors, materials, and styles.

faq.question: Can it search different languages?
faq.answer: Yes. The script accepts a language parameter for the Amazon browsing session.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it uses network access and a third-party API token.

faq.question: What should users avoid?
faq.answer: Users should avoid exposing API keys and should confirm that their product data collection follows applicable terms.
